imageCaption: Battle of Waterloo, William Sadler II, c. 1816. National Army Museum / public domain.

longForm.title: The Last Instrument: Napoleon's Operational Failure at Waterloo, June 1815
longForm.excerpt: The standard account of Waterloo attributes Napoleon's defeat to the Prussian arrival. This framing is incomplete in ways that matter. The more precise failure was operational, not tactical — and it began two days before the battle.
paragraphs: The received account of Waterloo attributes Napoleon's defeat to the Prussian arrival. The argument runs: Wellington held the ridge at Mont-Saint-Jean through the afternoon; Blücher's army arrived on Napoleon's right flank in late afternoon; the combination was decisive. This framing, which appears in accounts ranging from Victor Hugo's novel to standard campaign histories, is not wrong. It is incomplete in ways that matter for understanding what the battle reveals about the limits of Napoleonic operational method by 1815.

Napoleon began the battle at approximately 11:30 in the morning on June 18, 1815 — roughly three hours after most military historians believe he intended to start. The standard explanation is that he waited for the ground to dry after the previous night's heavy rain, since wet soil would slow his artillery movement and reduce cavalry effectiveness. This is plausible. Andrew Roberts, in Napoleon the Great (2014), and David Chandler, in The Campaigns of Napoleon (1966), both accept the meteorological explanation while noting that the three-hour delay gave Blücher's army three additional hours to close the distance from Wavre — approximately 15 kilometers east — to the right flank of the Waterloo position. The delay was operationally consequential regardless of its cause.

Wellington's defensive position on the reverse slope of the Mont-Saint-Jean ridge has been exhaustively analyzed, and the analysis consistently reaches the same conclusion: it was close to optimal for a commander who intended to fight defensively and expected relief from an allied force. Wellington knew, having met with Blücher at Quatre Bras two days before, that the Prussians would march to his assistance provided he could hold at Waterloo. His deployment — infantry behind the ridge crest to reduce exposure to French artillery, the chateau of Hougoumont anchoring the right, La Haie Sainte farmhouse anchoring the center — was a holding position, not a position designed to win the battle independently. The question Wellington had to answer was how long it could hold. His estimate was until the Prussians arrived, if the Prussians moved quickly.

The first phase of the battle tested Wellington's right flank. Jerome Bonaparte's division attacked the Hougoumont chateau complex beginning around 11:30 AM, in what Napoleon intended as a secondary action to draw Wellington's reserves southward before d'Erlon's main assault on the center. Hougoumont did not fall. The garrison of British Guards, later reinforced, held the buildings and wall for the duration of the battle. What the Hougoumont engagement reveals is a pattern: limited French objectives at the tactical level consumed disproportionate French resources. Jerome committed four brigades to a position Napoleon had designated as a demonstration. This is not the mark of an operationally coherent army.

D'Erlon's corps attack on the Anglo-Dutch center came at approximately 1:30 PM. Four infantry divisions deployed in column — a formation that has generated substantial historical argument. John Keegan's analysis in The Face of Battle (1976) describes the d'Erlon columns as exceptionally dense and deep, making them unable to generate adequate firepower to suppress defending infantry while also too compact to maneuver under fire. The columns were met by effective musketry from British infantry deployed in two-deep line, then charged by two brigades of heavy cavalry — Ponsonby's Union Brigade and Somerset's Household Cavalry. The charge was tactically decisive and operationally reckless: both brigades overran the French guns and were then cut up by French lancers when they failed to halt and reform. D'Erlon's attack was repulsed with heavy losses. The cavalry that repulsed it was effectively destroyed in the process.

Ney's cavalry charges against the Anglo-Dutch infantry between approximately 3:30 and 6:00 PM are the most analyzed phase of the battle, and the analysis is almost uniformly damning. Ney committed approximately 9,000 cavalry — the bulk of Napoleon's mobile reserve — against infantry squares on the ridge crest, without the artillery preparation or infantry support that would have given the charges any reasonable prospect of success. The squares held. Cavalry cannot break infantry squares that maintain formation and fire discipline, which Wellington's infantry did throughout. The question historians debate is not whether Ney's decision was wrong — it demonstrably was — but whether it reflected individual incompetence, deteriorating army morale, or a broader operational logic that had run out of options. Rory Muir's Tactics and the Experience of Battle in the Age of Napoleon (1998) suggests the charges had a certain rationality within a depleted operational menu: Ney had lost the infantry assault and was attempting to force a decision before the Prussians closed.

The Prussian arrival changed the French operational situation fundamentally. By approximately 4:30 PM, lead elements of Blücher's IV Corps under General Friedrich Wilhelm von Bülow began engaging the French right at Plancenoit, a village southeast of the main battlefield. Napoleon had to detach part of his reserve, including elements of the Old Guard, to hold Plancenoit against the Prussian advance. By evening, two Prussian corps were engaged against the French right while Ney was feeding resources into the front. The logistical condition of the French army by 7:00 PM — the Guard cavalry committed, d'Erlon's corps shattered, the cavalry reserve exhausted — made the Old Guard attack that followed not a stroke of tactical genius but the last military instrument available.

The Old Guard's attack at approximately 7:30 PM has been described, in most popular accounts, as Napoleon's final gamble. The analytical description is more prosaic: it was an attack by approximately 3,500 to 4,000 troops of the Imperial Guard's Middle Guard, conducted in column, against a position that had not been sufficiently softened by artillery and that was defended by Wellington's remaining infantry reserves. General Peregrine Maitland's 1st Foot Guards, lying behind the ridge crest, rose and delivered volley fire at close range. The Guard, accustomed over twenty years of campaigning to being decisive, gave ground. The retreat of the Guard — visible to the entire French army — triggered a collapse of army morale in a way that specific tactical defeats had not. The famous cry — possibly la Garde recule — marked the operational end of the battle.

The complete disintegration of the French army in the hours after the Guard's repulse is an operational phenomenon that the battle's tactical history tends to pass over quickly. Formations that had maintained discipline through hours of fighting dissolved into a rout within minutes. The Prussian pursuit, led by Gneisenau, was aggressive and effective in a way that differed from Wellington's more deliberate advance. Napoleon fled in his carriage, abandoned it for a horse, and arrived in Paris on June 21. He abdicated for the second time on June 22. French casualties for the battle are estimated at approximately 41,000, including prisoners; Allied casualties were approximately 22,000, Prussian approximately 7,000. The campaign as a whole — from the opening at Ligny on June 16 to the final pursuit — lasted four days.

The operational question Waterloo raises — and which David Chandler's work engages most directly — is whether Napoleon in 1815 had lost the strategic-operational flexibility that distinguished his earlier campaigns. The standard explanation invokes illness, the deterioration of his corps commanders, and the erosion of army quality after the disasters of 1812 and 1813. These factors were real. What is equally relevant is the operational structure of the campaign itself: Napoleon's decision to defeat the Anglo-Dutch and Prussians separately before they could concentrate was sound in principle but required execution faster than he achieved. At Ligny on June 16 he defeated Blücher; Grouchy's failure to maintain effective contact with the Prussians afterward allowed them to recover and march to Waterloo. The window between the Ligny victory and the Waterloo defeat was 48 hours. In the campaigns of 1806 and 1809, Napoleon had used such windows decisively. By June 18, 1815, something had changed — whether in the man, the army, or the odds, the evidence does not resolve.

shortForm.title: Tactical Victory, Strategic Catastrophe: The Tet Offensive of 1968
shortForm.excerpt: The Tet Offensive was a military defeat for the Viet Cong and a catastrophic political defeat for the United States. General Westmoreland called it a failure for the enemy. He was technically correct and strategically irrelevant.
paragraphs: The standard assessment of the Tet Offensive as a strategic American victory is defensible in military terms and largely irrelevant to its actual consequences. Beginning January 30-31, 1968, Viet Cong and PAVN forces launched coordinated attacks on 36 of 44 provincial capitals, five major cities including Saigon, and over a hundred district towns across South Vietnam. The tactical result was a substantial Viet Cong military defeat: most positions were retaken within days, the anticipated South Vietnamese popular uprising did not materialize, and Viet Cong infrastructure losses in the urban areas were severe. General Westmoreland called it a military failure for the enemy. He was technically correct and strategically irrelevant.

The intelligence failure preceding Tet was substantial and well-documented. Colonel Daniel Graham, then in MACV's intelligence directorate, acknowledged in a 1979 Army War College study that indicators of the attack were present but misread. ARVN forces had been granted holiday leave for the lunar new year; the conventional MACV assessment of Viet Cong capability held that they could not mount simultaneous nationwide operations. Both assumptions were wrong, and the evidence available before January 30 was sufficient to have questioned them. The CIA had produced assessments noting North Vietnamese preparations; they were not weighted against the MACV framework, which had been built around the premise that the enemy was under sustained attrition pressure and incapable of major offensive action. The attack on the U.S. Embassy compound in Saigon — which was entered briefly by Viet Cong sappers before being retaken — was not significant militarily. Its significance was that it was filmed.

The political consequences of Tet were irreversible within weeks. On February 27, 1968, Walter Cronkite delivered a CBS editorial calling the war a stalemate and stating that negotiation was the only rational path. Cronkite's broadcast is often cited in isolation; the more meaningful metric is what followed: Westmoreland's request for 206,000 additional troops, the refusal of that request by Secretary of Defense Clark Clifford's task force, the announcement by Lyndon Johnson on March 31 that he would not seek re-election, and the opening of peace negotiations in Paris in May. The military dimension of Tet — attack, repulse, heavy communist losses — was processed through a media and political environment that had already lost confidence in the official framework of the war. In that environment, the tactical outcome was almost beside the point.

sources.label: Tet Offensive — U.S. Army Center of Military History

onThisDay.headline: German Radio Announces Hitler's Death; Grand Admiral Dönitz Assumes Command
onThisDay.summary: On May 1, 1945, German State Radio broadcast that Adolf Hitler had died the previous evening fighting to his last breath against Bolshevism — concealing that he had died by suicide in the Führerbunker beneath the Reich Chancellery on April 30. Grand Admiral Karl Dönitz, named Reich President in Hitler's political testament, assumed command of the collapsing German state. Germany's unconditional surrender followed on May 7-8, 1945.
